one_line_takeaway: Repurposing knowledge-editing algorithms as unlearners, ROME+Dummy and IKE+Incorrect beat all four TOFU unlearning baselines on forget quality (0.121 and 0.249 vs. sub-threshold baselines), but the same forgetting-vs-utility trade-off holds and no method reliably erases knowledge under the harder Avoidant target.
key_finding: Two editing configurations cross the 0.05 Forget-Quality threshold where NONE of the four traditional unlearning baselines do: ROME with the Dummy target (Forget Quality 0.121) and IKE with the Incorrect target (Forget Quality 0.249). However both pay a utility cost -- ROME Dummy collapses Model Utility to 0.153 (vs. 0.614 ground truth), while IKE Incorrect keeps utility at a more usable 0.473, making IKE-Incorrect the best forgetting-while-retaining setting. WISE preserves Model Utility across the board (e.g. 0.610 on Dummy) but essentially fails to forget (Forget Quality ~4E-21) because it stores edits in a side memory that is not applied during generation, so its outputs remain the ground truth. ROME degrades catastrophically (emitting gibberish) on the more complex Incorrect and Avoidant targets. No method achieved sufficient forgetting on the Avoidant target. Conclusion: no single algorithm/target wins; the choice must follow the use case, and the forget-quality/utility trade-off persists just as in traditional unlearning.
core_contribution: The paper repurposes three existing knowledge-editing algorithms (ROME, IKE, WISE) for machine unlearning in LLMs by defining edit *removal* targets rather than replacement targets. The authors' own contributions are: (1) casting model editing as an unlearning mechanism and empirically comparing it against traditional TOFU unlearning baselines; (2) designing three unlearning target definitions -- 'Dummy' (non-answer), 'Incorrect' (a perturbed wrong answer), and a novel 'Avoidant' target generated by GPT-4 that acknowledges the topic without revealing the withheld fact; and (3) showing that editing-based unlearning can exceed traditional unlearning baselines on quality of forgetting in specific settings, while still exhibiting the same forget-quality vs. model-utility trade-off. Note: ROME, IKE, WISE, MEMIT, SERAC, MEND, SISA, RMU/WMDP are all *cited prior work*; the paper's original piece is applying editing to unlearning and the target definitions, especially the Avoidant one.
threat_model: Motivating goal (not an adversarial evaluation in this paper): a model owner wants to remove specific unwanted knowledge -- private/personal data (right-to-be-forgotten) or hazardous knowledge (the cited WMDP biosecurity/chemical/cyber setting) -- from a deployed LLM without full retraining and without degrading unrelated capabilities. The paper explicitly frames incomplete removal, collateral capability degradation, and 'misuse for model manipulation' as risks in its Impact Statement. It does NOT itself run extraction/rephrasing attacks against the edited models (it cites Patil et al. 2023 for that); forgetting is measured statistically via TOFU's distributional test, not via adversarial recovery. So the security framing is genuine (unlearning for privacy/safety) but the paper is an investigational benchmark study rather than an attack-or-defense paper.
limitations: Single model (Llama-2-7B) and a single fictitious benchmark (TOFU/Forget 10) at one forget ratio, so generality to real private/hazardous knowledge and larger models is untested. Forgetting is measured only by TOFU's distributional KS test -- there is no adversarial extraction, rephrasing, or logit-lens probing to verify knowledge is truly gone (the paper itself cites Patil et al. that deleted content can persist in hidden states). WISE's failure is partly an artifact of it not applying edits at generation time, a framework limitation rather than a fair algorithmic comparison. ROME's failures were tuned only over learning rate and clamp-norm factor. Some cells are missing (ROME Incorrect/Avoidant utility and several probability/truth-ratio entries) because ROME produced no usable outputs. Baseline numbers are taken from Maini et al. rather than re-run, and IKE does not modify weights, so its 'forgetting' is context-dependent and leaks under latent-knowledge access.

datasets_used: TOFU (fictitious-author unlearning benchmark; edits created for every prompt in the Forget 10 split = 10% of TOFU prompts)
TOFU sub-datasets for utility: Retain Set, Real Authors, Real World
ZsRE (used only to validate editing fidelity against EasyEdit's reported numbers)
GPT-4-generated Avoidant-answer dataset (authors' own, built to match TOFU's construction resources)
Base model: Llama-2-7B; ground-truth reference is the Retain-90 Llama-2-7B model
quantitative_results: ROME Dummy: Forget Quality 0.121 (>0.05 threshold) but Model Utility 0.153 (ground truth 0.614)
IKE Incorrect: Forget Quality 0.249 (>0.05), Model Utility 0.473 -- best forget-while-retain setting
WISE Dummy: Model Utility 0.610 but Forget Quality 4.22E-21 (fails to forget; edits not applied at generation)
No traditional baseline exceeds 0.05 Forget Quality: KL 4.88E-33, Gradient Ascent 1.43E-22, Gradient Difference 1.12E-25, Preference Optimization 5.10E-17
Baseline Model Utility: KL 0.000, GA 0.000, GD 0.589, PO 0.538 (ground truth 0.614)

Forget-set ROUGE (lower=better forgetting): ROME Dummy 0.041, WISE Dummy 0.965, IKE Dummy 0.466; IKE Incorrect 0.424 (moderate surface overlap consistent with IKE emitting a structurally similar but factually wrong answer, yet it still forgets distributionally per Forget Quality 0.249)
EasyEdit-metric fidelity on ZsRE Llama-2-7B closely matched published EasyEdit numbers, e.g. ROME Reliability 0.9617 vs 0.9245, IKE Reliability 0.9963 vs 1.0000, WISE Reliability 0.81 vs 0.77
Editing metrics on TOFU Dummy target: IKE Reliability/Generalization/Locality 1.000/1.000/0.888; ROME 0.985/0.975/0.313; WISE 0.100/0.043/0.999
On Incorrect target ROME editing metrics collapse to ~0.046/0.046/0.043, confirming degradation on complex targets
